input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes, comprising motor and gearbox components, controlled by a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller, and applied in machining operations.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) utilizes SysML for system modeling across abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer). Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs describe components, interfaces, and connectors. Behavior modeling is enhanced through MathWorks Simulink, other simulation tools, and general-purpose programming languages like C++ and Java.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system utilizes real-time, data-driven representations of robot joint behavior through live-data linking to maintain continuous synchronization between physical and digital components.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The system implements Asset Administration Shells (AAS) and streams data via MQTT protocol for integration with external systems.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin employs Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML for multi-level abstraction (requirements, functional, logical, product/technical layers), utilizing ADLs such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs for component-based structural modeling. Behavioral aspects are described using MontiArc Automaton, SysML state machines, and dynamic simulations via MathWorks Simulink or C++/Java. Data sources originate from physical systems through one-way digital shadow data flows, with contextual data traces aggregated and abstracted for specific purposes. Model relationships are defined through component interfaces, interconnections, and composition, integrating heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The document describes an automated one-way data flow from physical to digital objects as a characteristic of digital shadows but does not specify data types, communication protocols, transmission frequencies, event triggers, data quality assurance, preprocessing steps, or data integration mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle stages encompass the development phase where modeling is structured across multiple abstraction levels including requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers. Methodologies such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT are employed to manage complexity and transition between these abstraction layers during system development.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering and development processes for Digital Twins utilize methodologies including RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT to handle system complexity across abstraction layers. These processes involve Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML for structural modeling, architecture description languages (ADLs) like ACME, AADL, and MontiArc for component & connector perspectives, and integration with simulation tools such as MathWorks Simulink for behavior modeling. Evolution management includes version control of models across heterogeneous tools and stakeholder perspectives.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system incorporates industry standards such as SysML for system modeling, AADL and ACME for architecture description, MontiArc for component-based modeling with behavior specification via Automaton or state machines, and utilizes MathWorks Simulink for simulation. Methodologies including RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT are applied to manage system complexity across abstraction levels (requirements, functional, logical, and product/technical layers), ensuring compliance with MBSE practices and interoperability.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: Data is streamed via MQTT protocol, and a physical safety cell is implemented to restrict the working range of the robotic system.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly specific and accurately captures the details of the physical system described in the source text. It correctly identifies the robot model, its axes, components, controller, and application context found in the Use Case section. Supported by quotes: 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', 'controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl', 'suitable for complex manufacturing tasks such as machining'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document,' which is accurate. The source text distinguishes between a Digital Shadow and a Digital Twin, noting that the Digital Shadow is a 'passive entity' that 'neither operates autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.' While the paper describes the shadow's capabilities (e.g., 'identification of joint friction' and 'visualization in a dashboard'), it does not describe specific services provided by a Digital Twin (which would include active control or optimization).

The extraction is excellent and directly supported by the text. Section II.A states that 'Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML' and lists the abstraction levels: 'requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer.' It also correctly identifies 'Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs' and behavior tools like 'MathWorks Simulink' and 'C++ or Java'.

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],

and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe

ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML

Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s

are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an

modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from

shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extracted characteristic accurately reflects the source text, which describes digital shadows as 'real-time, data-driven representations' and the methodology as involving 'live-data linking' and 'live measurements'. The introduction explicitly states the approach leverages 'digital shadows — real-time, data-driven representations of robot joint behavior'. While the description is correct and specific, it could be more detailed regarding specific synchronization frequencies or latency requirements mentioned in the prompt's definition.

The document focuses on a single digital shadow instance for the first axis of a specific robot. It does not discuss multiple digital twin instances, centralized vs. decentralized architectures, or coordination between multiple instances. Therefore, the claim that the characteristic is 'Not in Document' is correct, as the source text does not contain this information.

The paper describes the architecture of the specific digital shadow implementation (using AAS, SysML, simulation models, and a dashboard) but does not describe 'overall orchestration and coordination of the Digital Twin system' in terms of system-wide patterns or constellation management as defined in the characteristic description. It details a specific instance rather than a constellation or system-wide orchestration. Thus, the 'Not in Document' claim is accurate regarding this specific definition.

The extraction correctly identifies the use of 'Asset Administration Shells (AAS)' and 'MQTT protocol'. The abstract states the methodology involves 'implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS), and live-data linking'. Section IV.A mentions that sensor data 'are streamed via MQTT'. This matches the extraction perfectly.

block_6_judge.reasoning: The provided source text discusses digital shadows, friction models, and data traceability for a robot. It does not contain any information regarding data ownership policies, privacy protection, regulatory compliance, consent management, or data anonymization. Therefore, the statement 'Not in Document' is accurate.

The extracted description accurately cites the MQTT protocol and the safety cell. The source text confirms: 'time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT' and 'The safety cell of the robot restricts the maximum working range'. These points directly support the extracted characteristic.
